title: Phymatosorus grossus
intro: Phymatosorus grossus, also known as the 'tree fern', is a species of plant in the family Polypodiaceae, characterized by its large, terrestrial fern-like appearance. The plant is used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antiulcer properties, making it useful in the treatment of fever, wounds, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Phymatosorus grossus include flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Phymatosorus grossus is often prepared as a decoction or a crude drug, used to treat various health conditions, including fever, diarrhea, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Phymatosorus grossus is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Polypodiaceae, commonly known as the polypody ferns. It is native to tropical Asia and is often found growing on trees and rocks. This species has been reclassified several times over the years, but it is currently recognized as a distinct species. Phymatosorus grossus is a type of epiphytic fern, which means it grows on other plants for support. Its classification is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Division: Pteridophyta, Class: Polypodiopsida, Order: Polypodiales, Family: Polypodiaceae, Genus: Phymatosorus, Species: P. grossus.
